title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
NoteKey Ideas



By the end of this tutorial you should be able to:

Define measurement error
Describe how classical measurement error influences treatment, conditioning, and outcome variables differently
Apply instrumental variables and 2SLS to recover unbiased estimates
Operationalize constructs as latent variables
Ensure measurement models meet specific invariance requirements

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
1. Measurement Error: Definitions & Types
Measurement error is the misalignment between the true state of a variable and its reported state. We often assume that the variables we reason about are the variables we observe. This assumption rarely holds in practice. Instead, we observe some proxy variable i.e., a noisy or imperfect stand-in for the true variable of interest.
Understanding measurement error is important because the variable we draw conclusions with is not always the variable we draw conclusions about. To identify unbiased causal estimates, we need to understand the relationship between the variable of interest and a noisy proxy.

1.1 Motivating Example: Subjective Well-Being
Researchers are studying the effect of happiness on physical activity. Because they cannot measure partipants’ happiness directly, they use a self-reported to determine happiness. The researchers believe that the self-reports, though not a perfect representation of happiness, correlate with partipants true happiness levels.





  1. In most ways my life is close to my ideal.
  
  Strongly disagreeStrongly agree



  2. The conditions of my life are excellent.
  
  Strongly disagreeStrongly agree



  3. I am satisfied with my life.
  
  Strongly disagreeStrongly agree



  4. So far I have gotten the important things I want in life.
  
  Strongly disagreeStrongly agree



  5. If I could live my life over, I would change almost nothing.
  
  Strongly disagreeStrongly agree



  
    —
    Answer all five items to see your score
  
  
  535


Diener, E., Emmons, R. A., Larsen, R. J., & Griffin, S. (1985). The Satisfaction with Life Scale. Journal of Personality Assessment, 49(1), 71–75. Free to use for research and educational purposes with attribution.










NoteNote



Prior work shows that single scale surveys correlate with true metrics at roughly \(r = 0.5\), while multi-item scales reach about \(r = 0.8\). Neither is perfect!




1.2 Classical Measurement Error
Classical measurement error occurs when the observed variable equals the the true value plus some noise:
\[D^* = D + U\]
where the error term \(U\) satisfies four key assumptions:

\(\mathbb{E}(U) = 0\) (zero mean)
\(U \perp D\) (independent of the true value)
\(U \perp Y\) (independent of the outcome)
\(U \perp Z\) (independent of any covariate)

The DAG below encodes the independence assumptions.











D

D



Y

Y



D->Y





Ystar

Y*



Y->Ystar





U

U



U->Ystar








Figure 1: We observe Y* (the proxy) but we are interested in Y.





There are other types of measurmemnt error (e.g., differential). We will focus on classicial measurement error throughout this tutorial.






NoteQuestion



Can a binary variable have classical ME? Why or why not?








TipAnswer





No. Classical ME requires \(U \perp Z\), but if \(Z\) is binary (\(Z \in \{0, 1\}\)), the error term is necessarily correlated with \(Z\). For example, since \(U = Z* - Z\), if Z is 1 then Z* must be 0 and U is -1, and if Z is 0 then Z* must be 1 and U is 1.




Other Examples of Classical ME

Blood pressure recorded at a single clinic visit
Self-reported hours worked
Human error when reading time from a clock
Mechanical error from scales

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
2. Causal Estimand, Identification & Attenuation Bias

2.1 Setup
Throughout the tutorial, we are interested in measuring the average treatment effect (ATE) of \(D\) on \(Y\). Most of our results assume a linear regression model. Below, we explain how measurement error in treatment, conditioning, and outcome variables can influence findings.


2.2 Classical ME in Treatment: Attenuation Bias
We want to determine \(\beta\) in \(Y = \beta D + \varepsilon\), but we observe only \(D^* = D + U\). Plugging the proxy into OLS gives:
\[\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y,\, D^*)}{\text{Var}(D^*)} =
\frac{\text{Cov}(\beta D + \varepsilon,\; D + U)}{\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U)}
= \frac{\beta\,\text{Var}(D)}{\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U)} = \beta\lambda\]
where \(\lambda = \text{Var}(D) / (\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U))\) is the reliability ratio. In the derivation above, we leverage the classical ME assumptions that \(\text{Cov}(D, U) = 0\) and \(\text{Cov}(\varepsilon, U) = 0\).






NoteQuestion



In which direction is \(\hat{\beta}\) biased when there is classical measurement error in \(D\)? (Hint: what is the value of \(\lambda\)?)








TipAnswer





Because \(\lambda < 1\), the OLS estimate attenuates toward zero. For example, when \(\lambda = 0.5\), a true effect of \(\beta = 0.6\) appears as \(\hat{\beta} \approx 0.3\).









TipAttenuation Bias



Attenuation bias occurs when a regressor’s estimated coefficient is biased toward zero. Measurement error on treatment variables results in attenuation bias.


Explore the chart below to see how the reliability ratio affects our causal estimate. Notice that when the reliability ratio gets smaller, the fitted slope shrinks toward zero even as the true relationship stays fixed. In other words, the causal estimate is attenuated.




                        
                                            


Figure 2: Attenuation bias as reliability decreases. The sage line is the true slope; the purple dashed line is what OLS recovers from the noisy proxy.






2.3 ME in Outcome Variables
We still want to determine \(\beta\) in \(Y = \beta D + \varepsilon\). This time, we observe a noisy outcome \(Y^* = Y + U\). Plugging the proxy into OLS gives:
\[Y^* = \beta D + (\varepsilon + U)\]
The measurement noise is absorbed entirely into the composite residual \(\varepsilon + U\). Because \(U\) is independent of \(D\), the relationship between \(D\) and \(Y\) is unbiased. However, the residual variance increases from \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\) to \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon + \sigma^2_U\), which inflates standard errors and reduces \(R^2\).


Exercise: Outcome measurement error
The following exercise shows how analysis with a noisy measure of \(Y\) compares to analysis with the ground truth measure.
The gpa1 dataset (Wooldridge) records self-reported college GPA (\(Y\)) and weekly study hours (\(D\)) for 141 undergraduates. Self-reported GPA is subject to small rounding and recall errors. We treat the recorded GPA as ground truth and simulate a noisy proxy:
\[\text{GPA}^*_i = \text{GPA}_i + U_i, \quad U_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,0.3^2)\]


Load the data in R

library(wooldridge)
d <- na.omit(gpa1[, c("colGPA", "study")])
set.seed(5)
d$gpa_noisy <- d$colGPA + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 0.3)

Question 1. Compute the measurement error \(U = Y^* - Y\) (gpa_noisy minus colGPA). What is the mean and standard deviation?
err <- d$gpa_noisy - d$colGPA
mean(err)
sd(err)
Question 2. Check the correlation between colGPA and gpa_noisy.
cor(d$colGPA, d$gpa_noisy)
Question 3. Regress GPA on study hours using colGPA and then using gpa_noisy. Compare the slope coefficients, standard errors, and \(R^2\).
summary(lm(colGPA    ~ study, data = d))
summary(lm(gpa_noisy ~ study, data = d))






TipSolution





library(wooldridge)
d <- na.omit(gpa1[, c("colGPA", "study")])
set.seed(5)
d$gpa_noisy <- d$colGPA + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 0.3)

# error stats
err <- d$gpa_noisy - d$colGPA
mean(err)   # ≈ 0.0
sd(err)     # ≈ 0.1

# correlation
cor(d$colGPA, d$gpa_noisy)   # ≈ 0.80

# regression
summary(lm(colGPA    ~ study, data = d))
# slope ≈ 0.046,  SE ≈ 0.029,  R² ≈ 0.017

summary(lm(gpa_noisy ~ study, data = d))
# slope ≈ 0.046,  SE ≈ 0.036,  R² ≈ 0.011



The two slopes are identical because ME in the outcome does not introduce bias. The standard error increases and \(R^2\) falls. A noisier outcome means you need a larger sample to achieve the same statistical precision.


Simulation
Each point below is one observation plotted as (true \(Y\), observed \(Y^*\)). Drag the slider to add noise to the outcome variable. Notice that the mean stays near zero while the standard deviation grows.




                        
                                            


Figure 3: Classical ME in the outcome: true Y (x-axis) vs observed Y* (y-axis). The error mean stays ~0 while the SD grows with noise.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
2.4 Measurement Error in Conditioning Variables
We still want to determine the ATE of \(D\) on \(Y\). This time, we assume there is some confounding variable \(Z\). Unfortunately, We only observe a noisy \(Z^* =
Z + U\) where \(U_Z\) is classical ME (mean zero, independent of \(D\), \(Y\), and \(Z\)).
We want to determine \(\beta_Z\) in:
\[Y = \beta_D D + \beta_Z Z + \varepsilon_1 \]
But we only observe \(\beta_{Z^*}\) in:
\[Y = \beta_D D + \beta_{Z^*} Z^* + \varepsilon_2 \]
\(\beta_{Z^*}\) is biased towards zero as in the bivariate regression, but what happens to \(\beta_D\)? We can see directly from the DAG structure that \(\beta_D\) is biased: there is an open backdoor back from \(D\) to \(Y\) through \(Z\), even after conditioning on \(Z^*\):











Z

Z



Zs

Z*



Z->Zs


P(z*|z)



X

X



Z->X





Y

Y



Z->Y





U

U



U->Zs





X->Y








Figure 4: Classical ME in the conditioning variable: controlling for Z* instead of Z leaves the backdoor path X ← Z → Y open.






2.4.1 Bias in Multivariate OLS
What can we know about the direction of the bias and the relationship to \(\beta_Z\)? Let’s explore how classical ME in a single variable affects coefficients in a multivariate OLS. First, we will show that \(\beta_{Z^*}\) is biased towards zero. Then, we will show that \(\beta_{D}\) is biased in an unpredictable direction. Specifically, the bias in \(\beta_{D}\) depends on the direction of \(\beta_Z\).
Remember that the variation in \(Z\) left after removing the component of \(Z\) explained by \(D\) is the residual variance from regressing \(Z\) on \(D\):
\[\sigma^2_{Z|D} = \text{Var}(Z) - \text{Var}(\hat{Z}_{|D}) \leq \sigma^2_Z\]
The attenuation factor in the multivariate model becomes
\[\lambda_1 = \frac{\sigma^2_{Z|D}}{\sigma^2_{Z|D} + \sigma^2_U}\]
and \[\hat\beta_{Z^*} = \lambda_1\,\beta_Z\]
Because \(\sigma^2_{Z|D} \leq \sigma^2_Z\) and \(Z\) is correlated with \(D\), we have \(\lambda_1 \leq \lambda\). This relationship reveals that classicial measurement error in multivariate regression accentuates attenuation in the variable with ME.
Now, we will explore how this bias in \(\beta_Z\) affects \(\beta_D\). To do so, we need to decompose the bias in \(\hat\beta_{D} = \beta_D + \text{bias}\). Intuitively, we need to know the relationship between \(D\) and \(Z\) and the relationship between \(Z\) and \(Z^*\). The combination of these relationships tells us how much unaccounted confounding influences \(\hat\beta_D\).
Let \(\delta\) be the slope from the regression of \(Z\) (some confounding variable) on \(D\) (i.e., \(Z = \delta_0 + \delta D + \epsilon\)). Then:
\[\hat\beta_{D} = \beta_D + (\beta_Z - \beta_{Z^*}) \delta = \beta_D + (1 - \lambda_1)\,\beta_Z\,\delta\]






NoteExercise




What are the two components in the bias term \((1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta\)?
What does \(\delta\) represent? When is this term large?
What does \((1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\) represent? How does the sign of \(\beta_Z\) affect the direction of bias in \(\beta_D\)?



\(\delta\) tells us the strength of the relationship between \(Z\) and \(D\). \((1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\) is the bias introduced by measurement error. The bias term \((1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta\) is non-zero whenever \(D\) and \(Z\) are correlated (\(\delta \neq 0\)) and \(Z\) has a true effect (\(\beta_Z \neq 0\)). In other words, measurement error in a conditioning variable biases the treatment coefficient, and the direction of bias depends on \(\beta_Z\) and \(\delta\).
Explore the graph below to see how measurement error in a conditioning variable influence \(\beta_D\). The starting DGP is \(Y = \beta_D D + \beta_Z Z +
\varepsilon\) with \(\beta_D = 1\) and \(\beta_Z = 0.8\).
Try adjusting \(\beta_Z\) using the dropdown menu. What happens to \(\hat{\beta}_X\) when you flip the sign of \(\beta_Z\)? In which direction is \(\hat{\beta}_X\) biased for different values of \(\beta_Z\)?






NoteAnswer





The bias on \(\hat{\beta}_X\) is \((1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta\), so the direction of bias depends on the sign of \(\beta_Z\) and \(\delta\).







                        
                                            


Figure 5: ME in the conditioning variable. Use the dropdown to change β_Z in the DGP; drag the slider to change the reliability of Z*. Sage = true β_D = 1, purple = OLS estimate, lavender = theoretical prediction.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
3. Instrumental Variables (IV) & 2SLS
Below, we will show how using instrumental variables can recover unbiased estimates when there is (i) classical ME in the treatment variable and (ii) classical ME in a confounding variable.

IV for Classical ME in Treatment Variables
To recover \(\beta\) consistently despite noisy \(D^*\), we need a second proxy \(T\) for the true \(D\). The IV must satisfy three assumptions:

Exclusion: \(\text{Cov}(T, \varepsilon) = 0\) (\(T\) affects \(Y\) only through \(D\))
Exogeneity: \(U \perp T\) (measurement error in \(D^*\) is independent of the instrument \(T\))
Relevance: \(\text{Cov}(T, D^*) \neq 0\) (\(T\) is correlated with the mismeasured treatment)

Remember that we want to determine \(\beta\) in \(Y =
\beta D + \varepsilon\), but we measure a noisy \(D^* = D + U\). Then
\[\text{Cov}(Y, T) = \text{Cov}(\beta D + \varepsilon, T) = \beta \text{Cov}(D,
T) \]
\[\text{Cov}(D^*, T) = \text{Cov}(D + U, T) =\text{Cov}(D,
T) \]
so \(\hat{\beta} = \text{Cov}(Y, T) / \text{Cov}(D^*, T)\).
In the first derivation, we leverage the exclusion assumption; in the second, we leverage the exogeneity assumption.


Two-Stage Least Squares
In practice, we can implement the estimator as a two-stage OLS (2SLS):
Stage 1: regress \(D^*\) on \(T\):
\[\hat D^* = \hat\pi_0 + \hat\pi_1 T\]
Stage 2: regress \(Y\) on \(\hat D^*\):
\[Y = \alpha + \beta\,\hat D^* + \eta\]


Exercise: BMI and Blood Pressure
Below, you’ll use 2SLS to see how instrumental variables can recover \(\beta_D\). We will compare the results from a 2SLS regression to a regression with the ground truth treatment.
Suppose a researcher wants to quantify how much systolic blood pressure rises per unit increase in BMI. The researcher suspects that reported BMI is subject to measurement error due to scale calibration or rounding.
We treat the NHANES BMI column as ground truth and simulate two independent noisy readings:
\[\text{BMI}^*_1 = \text{BMI} + U_1, \quad U_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,4^2)\] \[\text{BMI}^*_2 = \text{BMI} + U_2, \quad U_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(1,\,10^2)\]
\(\text{BMI}^*_1\) is the mismeasured proxy while \(\text{BMI}^*_2\) is the instrument.

Step 0: Load data and simulate measurement error
library(NHANES)
d <- na.omit(NHANES[, c("BPSysAve", "BMI")])

set.seed(9)
d$bmi_star <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 4)   # noisy regressor X*
d$iv       <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 1, sd = 10)  # instrument


Step 1: OLS benchmarks
# Benchmark: OLS on true (unobserved) BMI
ols_true <- lm(BPSysAve ~ BMI, data = d)

# What we observe in practice: OLS on the noisy proxy
ols_fit  <- lm(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star, data = d)

summary(ols_true)
summary(ols_fit)


Step 2: 2SLS with ivreg()
library(AER)
iv_fit <- ivreg(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star | iv, data = d)
summary(iv_fit, diagnostics = TRUE)


Step 3: Compare all three
models <- list(
  "OLS (true BMI)"  = ols_true,
  "OLS (noisy BMI)" = ols_fit,
  "2SLS (ivreg)"    = iv_fit
)

results <- sapply(models, function(m) {
  b  <- round(coef(m)[2], 3)        # slope (2nd coef after intercept)
  se <- round(sqrt(diag(vcov(m)))[2], 3)
  c(estimate = b, se = se)
})

print(results)






TipExpected results









OLS (true BMI)
OLS (noisy BMI)
2SLS (ivreg)




BMI coefficient
0.644
~0.499
~0.644


SE
(0.028)
(0.024)
(~0.043)



OLS on the noisy proxy attenuates the coefficient from 0.644 to ~0.499. The reliability ratio is \(\lambda = 7.4^2 / (7.4^2 + 4^2) \approx 0.77\). The 2SLS recovers the true slope despite using noisy proxies.






IV for Classical ME in Confounding Variables
We can also use instrumental variables to recover unbiased effects when there is classical ME in a confounding variable.
Consider the structural model below, where \(Z\) is the latent (never-observed) confounder, \(Z^*\) is a noisy proxy for \(Z\), and \(\beta_0\) is the causal effect we want to estimate:
\[Y = \beta_0 D + \beta_2 Z + U_Y\]
\[D = \beta_1 Z + U_D\]
\[Z^* = \beta_3 Z + U_{Z^*}\]
We can express \(\beta_0\) in terms of the variances and covariances of the observed variables.
After some algebra, we get
\[ \beta_0 = \frac{\text{Cov}(D,Y) -
\text{Cov}(D,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,Y)  /  \beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}{\text{Var}(D)
- \text{Cov}^2(D,Z^*) / \beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)} \tag{1} \]
but we don’t observe \(\beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z)\). With the second proxy \(V\) we can identify:
\[\beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z) = \text{Cov}(D,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,V) / \text{Cov}(D,V)
\tag{2} \]






TipWhy does \(\beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z) = \text{Cov}(D,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,V) / \text{Cov}(D,V)\)





Structural equation for our instrument: \(V = \beta_4 Z + U_V\). We can derive the following covariances:
\[\text{Cov}(D, Z^*) = \beta_1 \beta_3 \text{Var}(Z)\] \[\text{Cov}(D, V) = \beta_1 \beta_4 \text{Var}(Z)\] \[\text{Cov}(Z^*, V) = \beta_3 \beta_4 \text{Var}(Z)\]
So we get
\[\frac{\text{Cov}(D, Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*, V)}{\text{Cov}(D, V)} = \frac{\beta_1 \beta_3 \text{Var}(Z) \cdot \beta_3 \beta_4 \text{Var}(Z)}{\beta_1 \beta_4 \text{Var}(Z)}= \beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z) \tag{2}\]



Substituting (2) into (1) yields an estimator that depends only on observed covariances:
\[\beta_0 = \frac{\text{Cov}(D,Y)\,\text{Cov}(D,V) -
\text{Cov}(Y,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,V)}{\text{Cov}(D,V)\,\text{Var}(D) -
\text{Cov}(D,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,V)} \tag{3}\]






NoteQuestion



Without a second proxy \(V\), equation (1) cannot be estimated from observed data alone. What quantity do we need to know to estimate equation (1) without a proxy?








TipAnswer





We need to know \(\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)\). We don’t observe this quantity because \(Z\) is unobserved.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
4. Methods for Latent Variables
Sometimes the outcome refers to constructs that are not measured directly but can be quantified using observed attributes or indicators. In this section, we will show how researchers operationalize constructs of interest using multiple observed indicators (e.g., questions in a test or a survey).
During implementation, researchers must make several decisions based on substantive domain knowledge: (i) selecting the best indicators to model the latent variable of interest, and (ii) summarizing the information from those indicators. In applied research, researchers often rely on a composite score that quantifies the degree to which a construct is manifested for each unit of observation (e.g., sum scores, predicted scores, or imputed scores). Causal inferences can be more or less biased depending not only on the chosen scoring method but also on other research conditions, such as the number of indicators and the magnitude of their measurement error.
The latent variable approach assumes that the indicators share a common cause, allowing us to use the common variance to model an underlying unobserved variable by specifying a measurement model. This measurement model represents how observed variables are linked to a latent variable. Additionally, structural models can represent how observed variables (outside the measurement model) and latent variables connect to one another. This framework empirically contrasts substantive knowledge with data using structural equation modeling (SEM; Bollen, 1989), in which multiple equations are estimated simultaneously.
Below, we present a simplified example of incorporating a measurement model specification within the SEM approach.

Case 1: Observed Treatment to Latent Continuous Outcome
In this baseline scenario, the treatment condition \(X \in \{0, 1\}\) is perfectly measured and assigned, but the outcome is a latent construct \(\eta_Y\) measured by a set of continuous indicators.
By modeling the outcome as latent, the causal estimand (ATE) is evaluated on the true latent scale. This avoids the attenuation bias inherent in traditional two-step scoring strategies (such as using sum scores or extracted factor scores as an observed outcome in a standard regression).

1. Measurement Model Specification
For an individual \(i\) and item \(j\), the measurement model is:
\[y_{ij} = \nu_j + \lambda_j \eta_{Yi} + \epsilon_{ij}\]
Where the item residual is \(\epsilon_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \theta_j)\).
To identify the model and set the metric of \(\eta_Y\), we can either (i) standardize the latent variable prior to estimating the structural paths (\(\eta_{Yi} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \psi)\)) or (ii) fix a reference factor loading (e.g., \(\lambda_1 = 1\)).


2. Structural Model Specification
The structural parameterization directly regresses the latent variable on the observed binary treatment:
\[\eta_{Yi} = \alpha_Y + \tau X_i + \zeta_i\]
Where \(\zeta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \psi_{residual})\), and \(\tau\) represents the ATE on the latent scale.




Case 2: Latent Treatment Assignment
This second scenario maps onto selection mechanisms where an underlying latent propensity, \(\eta_X\), determines the true treatment assignment. This continuous propensity is discretized into an observed binary treatment \(X\), which subsequently causes the observed continuous outcome \(Y\).

1. Latent Propensity Measurement Model
The continuous latent condition is measured by a set of indicators \(k\):
\[\boldsymbol{x}_{ik} = \boldsymbol{\nu}_{Xk} + \boldsymbol{\lambda}_{Xk} \eta_{Xi} + \boldsymbol{\delta}_{ik}\]
Where \(\eta_{Xi} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) represents the underlying continuous condition. For example, when strict experimental conditions are difficult to meet, we might use a set of questions to determine whether participants are adhering to a specific treatment (e.g., dietary restrictions). We need sufficient information to classify them into the categorical conditions of interest. To do so, a threshold must be established.


2. Discretization and Treatment Assignment Mechanism
The latent condition dictates treatment assignment via an indicator function using a fixed threshold \(c\):
\[X_i = I(\eta_{Xi} > c)\]
(Note: \(X_i = 1\) if the condition is met, and \(X_i = 0\) otherwise).


3. Observed Outcome Model
The final structural model evaluates the effect of the assigned treatment on the observed continuous outcome:
\[Y_i = \alpha_Y + \tau X_i + \epsilon_i\]
Where \(\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_Y)\).

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
5. Measurement Invariance
Concerns may arise regarding whether scores (i.e., measures) derived from the latent variable framework are comparable across different groups. For example, we must assess whether the observed responses are caused by the same latent variable across groups, and whether that latent variable causes variation of the same magnitude for all indicators.
When utilizing a measurement model, we must ensure it meets specific invariance requirements. Essentially, this guarantees that the measurement properties do not vary because of group-related characteristics. Measurement invariance operates on a progressive spectrum, meaning a measure can demonstrate anything from weak to strict invariance depending on the parameters being constrained. Crucially, these levels are nested; if a lower level of invariance is not met, a higher level cannot be achieved. Below is a brief description of these levels, building from the lowest requirement:

Configural Invariance
This stage is designed to test if the model has the same basic form across groups, implying that the pattern of relationships between the indicators and the latent variable is equivalent. Achieving this level means the conceptual structure is reflected similarly across groups.


Metric Invariance
Once there is evidence that the model form is the same, the next step is to check for equivalence in the magnitude to which the indicators relate to the latent variable. This is tested by constraining the factor loadings to be equal across groups.


Scalar Invariance
This level of invariance implies that mean differences in the latent construct capture all mean differences in the shared variance of the indicators. For example, if we have an item asking about the frequency of a specific behavior, a scalar-invariant measure ensures that individuals from different groups with the exact same level of the latent trait will endorse the item with the same average frequency. A non-invariant measure would mean that for some groups, the average observed frequency is higher or lower without a corresponding true difference in the underlying latent variable.


Residual Invariance
The final step to establish measurement invariance is to test if the item residuals are equivalent. In other words, we constrain the specific variance and measurement error to be equal across groups. Although this level demonstrates strict invariance, it is not strictly required to compare latent means, since the residuals are distinct from the latent factor.
(Note: An alternative approach to establishing a measurement model falls under Item Response Theory (IRT), in which a probability function formalizes the relationship between a categorical observed response and a continuous underlying latent variable. The invariance property can also be tested within this framework, where a lack of invariance is more commonly referred to as Differential Item Functioning, or DIF.)

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
6. Takeaways

Before reasoning about confounding and generalizability, researchers often assume they measured experimental variables accurately.
This assumption is not always reasonable, especially when measuring abstract constructs or using instruments subject to human error.
Ignoring measurement error can bias outcomes even when there is no confounding present in the DAG
Well designed experiments (e.g., carefully selected instrumental variables) can help detect unbiased effects when researchers suspect measurement error.
We can use proxy variables as latent variables if we can model a valid relationship between the proxy and latent variable

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
Sources
An, L. (2025). Measurement in K-12 policy analysis (Doctoral dissertation, Harvard University). https://dash.harvard.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/7035e654-685f-4d1a-837e-e66ab492c665/content
Bulbulia, J. A. (2024). Methods in causal inference. Part 3: Measurement error and external validity threats. Evolutionary Human Sciences, 6, e42. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4723683/pdf/dyu272.pdf
de Romémont, J. (2024). Measurement in data science [Lecture notes]. https://uclspp.github.io/POLS0013/2-measurement-theory-and-error.html
Edwards, J. K., Cole, S. R., & Westreich, D. (2015). All your data are always missing: incorporating bias due to measurement error into the potential outcomes framework. International Journal of Epidemiology, 44(4), 1452–1459. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11588564/
